exercises.title: A: Word Formation
exercises.instructions: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space.

items.sentence: Despite being (1) ...... (DOUBT) at first, she eventually came to see the value of the (2) ...... (CONVINCE) evidence presented by the research team.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is dOUBT + CONVINCE, so the correct answer is "doubtful, convincing".

wrongOptions.1: The bare infinitive (1) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (1) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: Her (3) ...... (EXPLAIN) of the concept was so clear and (4) ...... (IMAGINE) that even students with no prior knowledge were able to grasp it immediately.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is eXPLAIN + IMAGINE, so the correct answer is "explanation, imaginative".

wrongOptions.3: The bare infinitive (3) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (3) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: The (5) ...... (ASSUME) that intelligence is fixed has been widely challenged, and scientists now argue that what determines potential is not genes but (6) ...... (DECIDE) action and consistent effort.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is aSSUME + DECIDE, so the correct answer is "assumption, decisive".

wrongOptions.5: The bare infinitive (5) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (5) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: Her initial (7) ...... (CONFUSE) about the topic gradually gave way to a deep (8) ...... (BELIEVE) that she could master it with enough practice.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is cONFUSE + BELIEVE, so the correct answer is "confusion, belief".

wrongOptions.7: The bare infinitive (7) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.sentence: It is almost (9) ...... (CONCEIVE) that a person of such (10) ...... (BRILLIANT) could choose to work in complete obscurity for so long.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is cONCEIVE + BRILLIANT, so the correct answer is "inconceivable, brilliance".

wrongOptions.9: The bare infinitive (9) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

exercises.title: B: Key Word Transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence:
The university introduced its new curriculum in 2020 and still uses it today. **SINCE**
The university ...... 2020.
items.answer: has been using its new curriculum since
acceptedAnswers: has been using its new curriculum since

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is since 2020, still ongoing, so the correct answer is "has been using its new curriculum since".

wrongOptions.been using its new curriculum since: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
They finally managed to think of a plan to deal with the problem. **COME**
They have finally ...... with the problem.
items.answer: come up with a plan to deal
acceptedAnswers: come up with a plan to deal

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is phrasal verb, so the correct answer is "come up with a plan to deal".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "come up with a plan to deal", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
His constant habit of talking over people in meetings really frustrates his colleagues. **ALWAYS**
He ...... in meetings, which really frustrates his colleagues.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is annoying habit, so the correct answer is "is always talking over people".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is always talking over people", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
The committee reached its conclusion only a few minutes ago. **JUST**
The committee ...... conclusion.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is just + present perfect, so the correct answer is "has just reached its".

wrongOptions.just reached its: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
The scientists haven't managed to figure out what caused the anomaly. **YET**
The scientists have ...... what caused the anomaly.
items.answer: not yet managed to figure out
acceptedAnswers: not yet managed to figure out

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is yet in perfect, so the correct answer is "not yet managed to figure out".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
It is now three hours since she started mulling over her decision. **BEEN**
She ...... for three hours.
items.answer: has been mulling over her decision
acceptedAnswers: has been mulling over her decision

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is duration, so the correct answer is "has been mulling over her decision".

wrongOptions.been mulling over her decision: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
I have never come across such an ingenious solution before. **FIRST**
This is ...... come across such an ingenious solution.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is first time structure, so the correct answer is "the first time I have".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
She's decided what to do about the offer and doesn't need more time. **MADE**
She ...... the offer.
items.answer: has already made up her mind about
acceptedAnswers: has already made up her mind about

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is decided, so the correct answer is "has already made up her mind about".

wrongOptions.already made up her mind about: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

exercises.title: C: Idioms / Collocations
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: In a fast-moving debate, you really need to have your ...... about you to respond quickly and effectively.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is stay alert, so the correct answer is "wits".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "wits" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "In a fast-moving debate, you really need to have your ...... about you to respon…".

items.sentence: She felt completely in the ...... about the team's plans until she finally asked the manager directly.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is uninformed, so the correct answer is "dark".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: When she spotted the familiar handwriting on the envelope, she immediately ...... two and two together.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is deduced, so the correct answer is "put".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: He has always been quick on the ...... – he grasps new ideas before most people have even finished reading the instructions.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is learns fast, so the correct answer is "uptake".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: The name doesn't ...... a bell – are you sure we've met before?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is sounds familiar, so the correct answer is "ring".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The sheer volume of material she was expected to learn in a week was enough to drive anyone ...... the bend.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is drive crazy, so the correct answer is "round".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence: After the exam period, she decided to ...... stock of how well she had done and think about what she could improve.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is evaluate, so the correct answer is "take".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Stop ...... hairs and give me a straight answer – do you think the plan is workable or not?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is minor details, so the correct answer is "splitting".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: I haven't spoken French in years, so I really need to
items.sentenceAfter: up on it before my trip.
items.originalSentence: I haven't spoken French in years, so I really need to **brush / cram / pick** up on it before my trip.
items.completedSentence: I haven't spoken French in years, so I really need to brush up on it before my trip.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "brush" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "brush" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "cram" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.cram: "cram" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("up on it before my trip.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.cram: "cram" doesn't fit here → "brush" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("up on it before my trip.").
optionContrast.pick: "pick" doesn't fit here → "brush" does, because Only "brush" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

optionContrast.turn: "turn" doesn't fit here → "come" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("round to the idea of studying abroad, but eventually sh…").
optionContrast.get: "get" doesn't fit here → "come" does, because Only "come" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
